user_prompt: In `lib\WifiManager\WebServer.cpp` abd `lib\WifiManager\WebServer.h`, use `ESPAsyncWebServer`, have a initialization function that registers all server request handlers

the init function should iterate through all items in the list `web_assets_list` and use the same handler for each item. The handler itself will check the GET request parameters again to see which payload is to be served.
use a streaming reply method if we are worried about file sizes being too big for RAM.
the contents shouldn't need to be decompressed when sent, use the reply headers to notify the browser that the reply is compressed
agent_reply: - Registers one exact GET route per asset using the same handler.
- Adds `/` as an alias for `index.html` when it exists.
The shared handler looks up the requested URL again, finds the matching `web_asset_desc_t`, and sends the already-gzipped payload with:

It serves the compressed byte array directly with the libraryâ€™s byte-array response path, so it does not decompress or build a giant RAM buffer first. The upstream library describes `ESPAsyncWebServer` as an asynchronous HTTP server with exact/prefix/regex routing support, which matches this registration approach. ([github.com](https://github.com/ESP32Async/ESPAsyncWebServer/blob/main/README.md))
One small integration note: `WebServer::init()` exists now, but I did not call it from Wi-Fi activation yet.

user_prompt: add another handler for the url "/download_file", where there will be a parameter "file_name", simply allow the user to download any file from the microSD card
agent_reply: Added the microSD download route in [WebServer.cpp](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp).
- Checks that the microSD card is ready.
- Uses a chunked async response that reads the `FsFile` in pieces, so it does not buffer the full download in RAM.

user_prompt: in the same manner, add a "/delete_file" handler that deletes the specified file
agent_reply: It also logs successful and failed delete attempts.
It also logs successful and failed delete attempts.

user_prompt: now, add a handler for "/list_files.json", it serves json text, inside is an array of strings. Do not use `ArduinoJSON` for this, use the same chunked async response method as serving a large file, but instead of large chunks, each chunks is a file name. No need to sort, just however the iterator of the file system library provides is fine.
agent_reply: It returns a JSON array of root-level microSD file names, for example:
It does not use `ArduinoJson`. The response is streamed with `beginChunkedResponse()` and keeps only:
It skips directories and emits files in the order `SdFat` provides them. Filenames are JSON-escaped before sending.

commit_message: starting to add a web server

user_prompt: We have made a lot of progress, a lot of things are working the way I want
Walk through the documentation I wrote earlier, we might've deviated a lot. Warn me where the implementation does not match the written documentation, and tell me suggested changes
Note: I am thinking about making the device way more secure, and the `bluetooth.json` and `wifi.json` will eventually go away, so ignore things that involve that
agent_reply: 1. **The documented file-transfer/filesystem design no longer matches the code.**

The docs still say Wi-Fi provides an FTP server, the app treats onboard flash and microSD as separate disks, and LittleFS is used: [README.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/README.md:7), [file-system-and-ftp-server-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/file-system-and-ftp-server-design.md:1).
- no FTP implementation beyond a readme in [lib/FtpServer/readme.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FtpServer/readme.md:1)
- a microSD-only HTTP file server in [WebServer.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:376)
- that HTTP server is only visibly initialized by the webserver test, not normal firmware startup: [test_webserver.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_webserver.cpp:53)
Suggested change: decide the real transfer direction now.
- If HTTP/web UI is the intended path, update README and filesystem docs from â€śFTP + LittleFS + two disksâ€ť to â€śmicroSD file access over HTTP/web UIâ€ť and delete or mark FTP notes as obsolete.
- If FTP and onboard flash still matter, they are implementation gaps, not documentation details.
2. **Several documented Wi-Fi and UI workflows are still scaffolding.**

The docs describe AP mode, station selection, NTP sync, cloud upload selection/progress, file viewing, and RTC setting: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:3), [wifi-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/wifi-workflow.md:9).
- selecting a configured station, AP, cloud upload, and NTP action only logs: [main.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:530)
That would avoid the docs reading like finished product behavior while these surfaces are still stubs.
3. **The call-recording UI behavior has drifted from the written UI contract.**

The docs say the user can change call volume, mute/unmute the mic, and see a 3-pixel tally border that becomes dotted when local mic is muted: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:7). The Bluetooth workflow also says the recording screen shows mute status and output volume: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:19).
- exposes push-to-talk mode switching and **speaker** mute in [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:185)

- uses a fixed 10-pixel solid red frame in [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:21) and [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:403)
- has mic mute support in `AudioManager`, but I did not find GUI wiring for it: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1307)
- has volume APIs, but not a recording-view volume control: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1231)
Suggested change: choose one UI model.
- If the device is primarily push-to-talk on internal hardware, rewrite the docs around P2T and speaker mute.
- If mic mute and volume are still required call controls, add those controls and make the tally border encode mic state as documented.
4. **Recorder performance/file-format docs are slightly ahead of current behavior.**

The file-format doc says new recordings should pre-allocate roughly 1 GB and truncate afterward: [recording-file-format.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/recording-file-format.md:7). The dual-core note also assumes pre-grown `.rec` files: [dual-core-load-distribution.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/dual-core-load-distribution.md:113).

Current code has preallocation implemented but disabled by default: [conf.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:14), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:418).

Also, the doc says packets have a header and payload; the current on-disk implementation writes a **fixed-size** `file_packet_t` for normal `.rec` packets, including a fixed payload array and `payload_length`: [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:31), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:230).
- update the format doc to explicitly say `.rec` packets are fixed-size today
- say preallocation is optional/compile-time unless you intend to enable it again
5. **The high-level audio/hardware docs overstate two areas.**

The high-level design says an external WM8960 or SGTL5000 codec may be enabled by build flag: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:9). In the current audio manager, external I2S codec enable functions are explicit â€śnot implemented yetâ€ť stubs: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:626).

The same doc says CVSD local PCM is 8 kHz: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:31). The implementation accepts 8 kHz HFP, but `AudioFifo` upsamples 8 kHz input into its 16 kHz internal representation, and recorder packets are labeled `*_16KHZ_MONO`: [AudioFifo.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:90), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:315).
- state â€śexternal codec path planned, internal M5Stack audio path implementedâ€ť
- HFP CVSD ingress/egress can be 8 kHz
- recorder/internal FIFO normalizes to 16 kHz today

- Bluetooth connection auto-starts a call recording after entering a connected/audio-available state, matching the Bluetooth workflow: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:13), [main.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:166).

- The recording stop path can also disconnect Bluetooth: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:17), [RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp:64).

- Wi-Fi activation shuts down Bluetooth/audio/recording first, which matches the high-level design: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:17), [WifiManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:291).

- RTC-based recording naming and `.rec` output are in place: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:21), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:68).
- Top bar date/time and battery status are implemented: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:16), [FlyGui.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:630).

Ignoring the eventual removal of `bluetooth.json` and `wifi.json` as requested: the recording format already has security hooks, but they are placeholders right now. `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY` adds nonce fields and leaves encryption/authentication as TODOs: [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:39), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:206).
Before hardening transfer/config storage, I would write one short security design doc that states:
- how keys are provisioned and recovered
- what local UI actions are allowed without trust/authentication
- whether the HTTP file surface survives at all
I did not count `bluetooth.json` or `wifi.json` mismatches in the findings. I also did not run builds or tests for this pass; this was a documentation-to-code review.

user_prompt: Do we need to use SdFat? I don't need "grow_file" anymore, but I do want to keep the file attribute for file creation time.
agent_reply: Short answer: **if you want the firmware to keep using the FAT file creation timestamp and file attributes directly, keep `SdFat` for now.** `grow_file()` is no longer the deciding factor.
`SdFat` is currently giving you APIs the Arduino ESP32 `SD` wrapper does not expose:

The ESP32 Arduino `SD` stack does use FatFs underneath, and FatFs does write FAT creation time when it creates a file. In this installed toolchain that time comes from `time(NULL)` via FatFs `get_fattime()`, not from your `ClockAgent` RTC callback. ESP-IDFâ€™s FAT layer has timestamp support, but the Arduino `FS` facade exposes only `File::getLastWrite()`; its VFS stat path maps FAT modified time to `st_mtime` and leaves `st_ctime` as zero. So switching to Arduino `SD` would not give your application the same creation-time readback API you have now.
- If you want **PCs to see a sane created time on the SD card**, Arduino `SD` can probably work after you make sure the ESP32 system clock is initialized from RTC before file creation.
- If you want **your firmware to query creation time and FAT archive attributes**, Arduino `SD` is a downgrade unless you add low-level FatFs access yourself.
- Your current code does query those values, so a full switch away from `SdFat` would require redesigning `MostRecentFiles` and part of `DiskStats`.
There is also a migration cost beyond timestamps. These currently use `FsFile`/`SdFs` patterns:
1. **Keep `SdFat` as the one real microSD filesystem layer** for now.
2. Remove `grow_file()` and preallocation if you no longer want them.
3. Avoid the current test-only dual mount long term.
- write an Arduino `FS` adapter over your `SdFat` microSD layer, or
- choose an FTP library that supports `SdFat` directly.
That preserves your creation-time and FAT-attribute behavior while avoiding a storage-layer rewrite just to satisfy the FTP library.

user_prompt: look for `#ifdef BUILD_FTP_SERVER` and execute TODOs inside, any added code about the FTP server lives inside `#ifdef BUILD_FTP_SERVER` please
agent_reply: Done. I replaced the guarded FTP TODOs in the runtime path:

I put the FTP credentials and security note inside the guarded block in [WebServer.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:21). The note calls out that this is plain FTP, not SFTP, and the placeholder credentials need replacing before exposure.
- removed the test-local FTP startup/polling path
- made its idle loop poll the `WifiManager` instance, so the normal guarded runtime path drives FTP

One important detail: `WifiManager::poll()` has early returns in the connected/scan paths, so I placed the FTP poll at the top of the method. Leaving it where the TODO was would have skipped FTP servicing during your SoftAP webserver test.

- That build does **not** compile the guarded FTP additions right now because `BUILD_FTP_SERVER` is not currently defined anywhere in the repo. When you want FTP active, add `-DBUILD_FTP_SERVER` to the environment that should include it.
Done. I replaced the guarded FTP TODOs in the runtime path:

- removed the test-local FTP startup/polling path
- made its idle loop poll the `WifiManager` instance, so the normal guarded runtime path drives FTP

One important detail: `WifiManager::poll()` has early returns in the connected/scan paths, so I placed the FTP poll at the top of the method. Leaving it where the TODO was would have skipped FTP servicing during your SoftAP webserver test.

- That build does **not** compile the guarded FTP additions right now because `BUILD_FTP_SERVER` is not currently defined anywhere in the repo. When you want FTP active, add `-DBUILD_FTP_SERVER` to the environment that should include it.

user_prompt: add a helper that checks if the incoming message is authenticated, meaning:
* it has both a request key-value pair that is a `timestamp` with a string in the format of `YYYY-MM-DD-HH:mm:SS`
* the time is within +/- 2 minutes of current time
* and a hash of that string, another key-value pair 'hash', matches what the firmware generates with `Aegis::hmacSha256` and `Aegis::getMasterKey`
then add a file upload endpoint "/file_upload" that requires this, and also a "file_name", and also the file contents, and write the file to the microSD card.
agent_reply: - writes raw request body bytes to the microSD card
- also accepts multipart file chunks if the auth params are available before the file part
- upload body is plaintext; only the request is authenticated
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest` passes, and `git diff --check` passes.
- writes raw request body bytes to the microSD card
- also accepts multipart file chunks if the auth params are available before the file part
- upload body is plaintext; only the request is authenticated

commit_message: some work on secured web interactions

user_prompt: implement the C++ handler for "/set_cfg", it needs the same timestamp authentication helper, and it handles a large binary blob that is encrypted before decryption, reject it if it is over 64kb, decrypt it into something that ArduinoJSON can handle, then have it parse it into objects. Assume the frontend sends back an object with two main keys "network" and "bluetooth" similar to the JSON we've sent out. Use `BtHostList` and `WifiManager` functions to save them into NVS, implement functions as needed

Remember, we have agreed to operate on a monolithic blob of data, not streaming, even though the "/get_cfg" handler is streaming, you have recommended to change that one later to sending a monolithic blob as well.
A rule: a blank or missing wifi password means "do not change existing". As a policy, open wifi networks may not be used, and passwords are a bit "write only"
agent_reply: Implemented `/set_cfg` as a monolithic encrypted binary config upload.
- Decrypts into a PSRAM-preferred buffer, null-terminates it, then parses with ArduinoJson.
- Applies `"network"` and `"bluetooth"` objects into NVS via new manager commit helpers.
- blank/missing password preserves existing password by SSID
- new Wi-Fi entry without a password is rejected
- open/invalid-password Wi-Fi entries are rejected on network config updates
- Added disconnect cleanup so an aborted upload will not leave the config upload state stuck.
Build passed. I also restored the generated `src/version.c` change after the build. Unrelated dirty files like `.vscode/extensions.json` and the btramusage test files were left untouched.
Implemented `/set_cfg` as a monolithic encrypted binary config upload.
- Decrypts into a PSRAM-preferred buffer, null-terminates it, then parses with ArduinoJson.
- Applies `"network"` and `"bluetooth"` objects into NVS via new manager commit helpers.
- blank/missing password preserves existing password by SSID
- new Wi-Fi entry without a password is rejected
- open/invalid-password Wi-Fi entries are rejected on network config updates
- Added disconnect cleanup so an aborted upload will not leave the config upload state stuck.
Build passed. I also restored the generated `src/version.c` change after the build. Unrelated dirty files like `.vscode/extensions.json` and the btramusage test files were left untouched.

user_prompt: By policy I am only allowing one customizable AP configuration, I've change the header file to use `kNetworkConfigMaxEntriesAP`, `parse_network_wifi_array` requires a big edit since it needs to handle a length now
I might relax the requirement later so it's still an array
agent_reply: Done. I updated the Wi-Fi config parsing to respect per-table capacities:
- `/set_cfg` also enforces the AP array limit as `1`, while still keeping the JSON as an array for later relaxation

I also fixed the more dangerous side effect: `sanitize_network_config()` was still looping over `kNetworkConfigMaxEntries` for `access_point[]`, which would go out of bounds now that AP storage is only one slot.

One extra safety change: `loadFromNvs()` now queries the stored blob size first. If an older NVS blob has the old larger `network_cfg_t` layout, it logs and falls back to defaults instead of failing boot.
Build passed, and I restored the generated `src/version.c` change afterward.

user_prompt: This list needs to work with the bonded devices list (esp_bt_gap_get_bond_device_list)
If an entry exists in BtHostList but not esp_bt_gap_get_bond_device_list, it is kept for showing in the web front-end, but it does not show in the GUI ScrollView
If an entry exists in esp_bt_gap_get_bond_device_list but not in BtHostList, it does not show up on the web front-end nor the GUI ScrollView

On boot, and on submission of new configuration from web front-end, if an entry exists in esp_bt_gap_get_bond_device_list but not in BtHostList, then delete the bond with esp_bt_gap_remove_bond_device
Upon completion of bonding/pairing, an entry is entered into BtHostList
If the user edits a BDADDR to all 0 or blank in the web front-end, it means when it is submitted back, it is considered not a valid entry.
If the user selects to unpair a device through the touchscreen GUI, unpair it by deleting the bond, but do not remove the entry from BtHostList, as we might want to keep the customized name
Entries that are considered blank are never sent to the web front-end via the get_cfg request

Entries that are considered blank are never sent from the web front-end via the set_cfg request, but if encountered, they are skipped and do not enter the table, and that request will blank out the remainder of the table after processing the available JSON entries
I just wrote this policy, make sure the code follows this, do not remove the comment as the javascript side isn't implemented and I need to remember what the policy is
agent_reply: Implemented the policy and kept your comment in [BtHostList.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.h:4).
- Boot now calls `bt_host_list->pruneBonds()` after loading, so bonded devices not present in `BtHostList` are removed.
- Web config submission now prunes bonds through `replaceHostList()`, so omitted hosts also lose their bond.
- GUI unpair now calls a new `BtHostList::unpair()` instead of `remove()`, so the bond is deleted but the host entry/custom name stays.
- `getFirstPhone()` / `getFirstLaptop()` now only return bonded entries, matching the GUI visibility policy.
- `/set_cfg` Bluetooth parsing now skips blank or all-zero MAC entries, blanks the remainder of the staged table, and still rejects malformed nonblank MACs.
- `/get_cfg` defensively skips blank Bluetooth slots if storage ever gets weird.
- `sanitize_bt_host_list()` compacts valid entries and blanks the tail.
Build passed, and I restored the generated `src/version.c` change afterward.
Implemented the policy and kept your comment in [BtHostList.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BtHostList.h:4).

- Web config submission now prunes bonds through `replaceHostList()`, so omitted hosts also lose their bond.
- GUI unpair now calls a new `BtHostList::unpair()` instead of `remove()`, so the bond is deleted but the host entry/custom name stays.
- `getFirstPhone()` / `getFirstLaptop()` now only return bonded entries, matching the GUI visibility policy.
- `/set_cfg` Bluetooth parsing now skips blank or all-zero MAC entries, blanks the remainder of the staged table, and still rejects malformed nonblank MACs.
- `/get_cfg` defensively skips blank Bluetooth slots if storage ever gets weird.
- `sanitize_bt_host_list()` compacts valid entries and blanks the tail.
Build passed, and I restored the generated `src/version.c` change afterward.

user_prompt: change the "/get_cfg" handler to build up a large single string and then encrypt all of it in one chunk before sending it out
agent_reply: Implemented `/get_cfg` as a monolithic encrypted blob in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:2448).
- Builds the full config JSON into one `String`.
- Encrypts that whole JSON once with AES-GCM using `Aegis::getMasterKey()`.
- Keeps Wi-Fi/cloud passwords out of the JSON.
Build passed. I also restored the generated `src/version.c` churn after the build.

user_prompt: I have created empty files, `lib\WifiManager\WebCfgHandlers.cpp`, `lib\WifiManager\WebCfgHandlers.h`, `lib\WifiManager\WebFileHandlers.cpp`, and `lib\WifiManager\WebFileHandlers.h`

Server setup, management, authentication, can still use `WebServer`. I think you can understand how to handle what I haven't mentioned
agent_reply: Done. `WebServer.cpp` is now mostly auth, asset serving, routing, and FTP startup. Config endpoints moved into [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp), and file upload/download/delete/list endpoints moved into [WebFileHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebFileHandlers.cpp).

I also updated [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h) to expose the small shared helpers the handlers need: request auth, auth error names, JSON string escaping, request param lookup, and MAC formatting.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest` compiled the touched C++ files, then failed at link with the known IRAM0 issue:
I restored `src/version.c` afterward. The new handler files are still untracked in git, matching the fact that they were newly created files.

user_prompt: we can start stitching together the web front-end

the backend needs a handler for "/get_info" (implement in `WebServer.cpp`), requiring no authentication, sends an unencrypted JSON object, that fills in the `<div id="info">` inside `index.html`, the javascript `function body_onload()` will use ajax to retrieve this JSON object and fill in `<div id="info">`. On failure, keep retrying. On success, hide `<div id="loading">`

- Updated [package_web_assets.py](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/tools/package_web_assets.py:421) so PlatformIO inlines local CSS/JS into packaged HTML.

- On success, it fills `#info` and hides `#loading`.

the action of reset is not implemented yet, the handler for that action will also have a check, so having this in the front end is not a problem
use this boolean to show or hide `#password_reset_entry`, and change the `#password_reset_help` to say that a password reset requires the usage of the default soft AP mode

user_prompt: we are reworking how security works, there are now two keys, filecrypt-key and network-key, I want the filecrypt key to have limited exposure.
first, fill in `kSaltNetwork` with "36 22 5c 86 c1 70 f2 c1 11 d3 d6 df 57 6e 28 93 71 4e 52 6c d8 43 b2 6b df ee 1f 12 c9 a3 e5 b2 "

then extend `Aegis::init` to load two keys into memory, `hasMasterKey` should not be renamed and reflect if both keys are available. But `getMasterKey` and `setMasterKey` and similar getters and setters now need to reflect the names `filecrypt` and `network`
`setTestTempMasterKey` also needs to be split into two and `setTestTempPassword` needs to create both keys
agent_reply: - `Aegis::init()` now loads both `filecrypt_key` and `network_key` from NVS.
- `hasMasterKey()` still exists, and now returns true only when both keys are available.
- `setTestTempPassword()` now derives both keys using separate salts.
- Audio recording encryption now uses only the filecrypt key.
- Web auth/config encryption/cloud upload auth now use the network key.
- Updated `test_sdcard()` to install deterministic test keys for both slots.
Also made `Aegis::init()` open NVS read-only and close the handle after loading; setters open write access only when actually saving keys.
Build passed. I restored `src/version.c` after PlatformIO regenerated it.

user_prompt: we need a session-security-state type of structure to hold web browser session security information
only one session is allowed, even having two browser windows open is not allowed and will simply cause stuff to fail
* randomly generate 32 bytes, called the session-challenge, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-challenge, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-client, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-response-from-client, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-server, and remember this
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called the session-salt-from-server, and remember this
* the response from "/get_info" needs to include: session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
on the client (javascript), a similar object needs to track the session security information
the javascript ajax call into "/get_info" needs to:
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called session-salt-from-client, remember this, during the call into "/get_info", this is included
upon receiving a reply from "/get_info", which includes session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server, all of this needs to be remembered
at this point, the user has not entered in a password, we do not have the key to do anything, we cannot generate any of the responses
at some point, the user will enter in a password. When this happens:
* derive the network-key from the password (a copy of `kSaltNetwork` needs to be included in the javascript file)
* derive the session-response-from-client (from the session-challenge and network-key)
* derive the session-response-from-server (from session-response-from-client and network-key)
* validate the computed-session-response-from-server against the received-session-response-from-server
* if invalid, then the password is wrong or the server is malfunctioning
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* derive session-key from the network-key and session-salt
* use ajax to request "/get_cfg", included in the request header are: session-response-from-client, session-salt-from-client
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
* delete the entire `#login_panel` from the page, and blank out any variables that may contain the password. All keys are to be stored securely using WebCrypto if possible
The C++ backend needs to have "/get_cfg" modified to handle all of that
* validate session-response-from-client using network-key and session-challenge
* return error code if not validated
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* compute the session-key with the network-key and session-salt
* construct the reply with the information as JSON but as a gigantic string
* encrypt the gigantic string using session-key
When we get the data from "/get_cfg"
* if not authentic, stop and warn the user.
* fill the page elements with data from the JSON
delete `WebServer::authenticateRequest` and all usages of it, later when decryption is implemented, the decryption results will indicate if the request is authentic.
do tasks up to here so I have a chance to review, I'll issue another prompt for "/set_cfg", which would reuse a ton of what has been built up to this point
agent_reply: Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the deviceâ€™s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.
Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the deviceâ€™s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.

user_prompt: I know you just deleted it, but we need a `WifiApModeView` that is a `FlyGuiView`. This will display Wi-Fi credentials while in soft AP mode.
Place `sprit_wifi_50` in the top left corner (but not covering the top bar). If this is the default super safe randomly generated soft AP, then also put `sprite_security_50` beside it.

The bottom right corner, also linked to `BtnC`, put in a clickable `FlyGuiItem` that switches between `sprit_eye_closed_100` and `sprit_eye_open_100` when touched. Open eye means the info on the screen is displayed, closed means the sensitive parts are displayed as "**********"
Below the Wi-Fi icon, there should be 5 lines of text, similar to below
IP: <IP of the internal web server, probably 192.168.4.1>
Below the IP line, use the smallest font, show a line of text that cycles through a few displayed data items, cycling every 3 seconds, the items are:
* "page loads: n", number of times the "/get_info" request has been served since starting the AP
* "logins: n", number of times the "/get_cfg" request has been served since starting the AP, it is not displayed under security-level 0
* "saves: n", number of times the "/set_cfg" request (also the reset password and reset memory) has been served since starting the AP
* "errors: n", number of times there has been an apparent error, such as security-level mismatch error, or, some form of authentication error, those are the important ones
* "downloads: n", number of times a file has been downloaded, also track deletions and uploads with this number even though it doesn't make sense
`WifiManager` needs getters for these, and they need to be reset when the Wi-Fi starts, there may be request URL handlers missing but we'll implement those later

I am aware that I did not include some sort of stop button. The user must either click the physical reset button or power button to exit. So add a red line of text, small, as the next line, hinting so. I have no idea if it fits on the screen but do it so I can see.

* makes the domain "the-fly-<4 hex from MAC>" work as the URL, or something to that effect

user_prompt: does the change you just made effect how the ESPAsyncWebServer and AsyncTCP works?
agent_reply: Yes, but probably not the way youâ€™re most worried about.

`ESPAsyncWebServer` / `AsyncTCP` should still work at the TCP socket level because the new path still creates the ESP netif through `wifiLowLevelInit(false)`, applies `esp_wifi_set_mode(WIFI_MODE_AP)`, configures `WIFI_IF_AP`, then calls `esp_wifi_start()`. AsyncTCP listens through lwIP, not through `WiFi.softAP()` specifically.

The risky part is Arduinoâ€™s AP wrapper bookkeeping. Because we now bypass `WiFi.softAP()` / `WiFi.AP.begin()`, helpers like `WiFi.softAPIP()` may return `0.0.0.0` or stale data even though the AP itself is up. I already dodged that in the new log line, but I found existing uses here that may need the same treatment:

So: the web server should still accept connections by IP, but the UI/API may report the wrong AP IP unless we move those call sites to a lower-level AP IP helper too. Also, mDNS is the one â€śkeep an eye on itâ€ť item, since it can be sensitive to interface/event setup.
Yes, but probably not the way youâ€™re most worried about.

`ESPAsyncWebServer` / `AsyncTCP` should still work at the TCP socket level because the new path still creates the ESP netif through `wifiLowLevelInit(false)`, applies `esp_wifi_set_mode(WIFI_MODE_AP)`, configures `WIFI_IF_AP`, then calls `esp_wifi_start()`. AsyncTCP listens through lwIP, not through `WiFi.softAP()` specifically.

The risky part is Arduinoâ€™s AP wrapper bookkeeping. Because we now bypass `WiFi.softAP()` / `WiFi.AP.begin()`, helpers like `WiFi.softAPIP()` may return `0.0.0.0` or stale data even though the AP itself is up. I already dodged that in the new log line, but I found existing uses here that may need the same treatment:

So: the web server should still accept connections by IP, but the UI/API may report the wrong AP IP unless we move those call sites to a lower-level AP IP helper too. Also, mDNS is the one â€śkeep an eye on itâ€ť item, since it can be sensitive to interface/event setup.

commit_message: serving pages over soft AP works, had a hiccup about disk space retrieval too slow tripping the wdt

user_prompt: When a recording is finished inside AudioFileRecorder::stopRecording , use CallManager to generate a chunk of text to write into the file with AudioFileRecorder::queueMetaText if the recording was a call. If it was a memo, figure out what RecordingView says is the memo-type, use AudioFileRecorder::queueMetaText to make it say â€śmemo-type:<x>â€ť. If possible, rename the file so that the first letter captures what the memo type was.
agent_reply: The recorder now queues final metadata from inside `AudioFileRecorder::stopRecording`: calls get a `CallManager`-formatted text block, and memos get `memo-type:<x>`. The recording view now reports memo-type changes to the recorder, and stopped memo files are renamed after close so the first filename letter matches the final memo type when possible. Promoted memo-to-Bluetooth recordings are marked as call recordings.

Note: `pio run -e thefly` still fails because that env does not define `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL`; that is unrelated to this change. `src/version.c` was already dirty before I started, and the build script regenerated its hash.
